body_txt: A Very Special Episode with a moral about a very important issue: children must be careful around all strangers and should not follow, talk to, or accept things from strangers. Of course, being children's TV shows, these topics have to be presented in an easy-to-understand approach without being scary or overly detailed. They might not mention crimes by name, but rather stick to general descriptors or using this type of phrasing, "being touched in a way that makes you feel uncomfortable," sometimes due to censorship or to use child-friendly language. So, expect the most graphic euphemisms you can get onto suitable-for-all-ages television, such as the "bathing suit area". For these reasons, the bulk of the media tends to be either about "safety tips" like never talking to strangers (which usually refers to strange adults but not always) and rejecting all forms of generosity, or about a Card-Carrying Villain kidnapper and the more fantastical things he does to his victim . It's also worth noting that in the vast majority of child kidnappings and sexual abuse cases — believed to be around 80% according to various estimates — the abuser is not a stranger but someone the child knows and trusts, usually because they have easy access to the child, have more inherent authority they can use to take advantage of them, and, in custody cases (which make up almost all kidnappings) the kidnapper has something to gain by kidnapping the child. Worse still, of that 80%, the majority of it is committed by the victim's parents. However, that doesn't contradict that kids shouldn't trust strangers and familiar adults alike, and it doesn't negate the many cases of children being victims to their teachers, doctors, etc., but also with the modern twist of kids being on social media, thus making them easily accessible targets according to The Exodus Road in their 2025's human trafficking report . Since this Aesop was founded in old times, the information it was based on would be outdated and inaccurate by modern standards, and its execution could have been clumsy and not holistic in addressing the whole issue, which explains why it's rarely played straight and the attitudes about it have changed as well. However, as tropes evolve with time and maybe due to Tropes Are Flexible , some creators have picked up on this following recent data and presented this aesop in a contemporary and up-to-date method, even involving the internet and its role in this for child users. These days, experts advise caregivers to warn their children against suspicious actions and signs rather than on whether you know someone. And Heaven have mercy on your psyche if the writers decide to disregard the "don't scare the crap out of the kids" part . Compare Drugs Are Bad , another favorite kids-show message in the 1980s. See also, Memetic Molester , for fictional characters who are portrayed in a way that makes it a good idea for them to stay away from kids. The plot may involve a Red Riding Hood Replica , as a familiar tale about an innocent child who encounters a conniving predator. Compare and contrast Internet Safety Aesop , the stock lessons for the modern age about not trusting strangers online.
